# ARC-QUIC-CRT-0019 - QUIC CRT client-role 1-RTT readiness architecture

## Purpose

Describe how the client-role Finished path publishes the first application packet-protection material only after peer Finished verification, so the supported active-loopback data-path can open honestly without widening the supported surface.

## Requirements Satisfied

- REQ-QUIC-CRT-0121

## Design Summary

The client Finished path now has a narrow, proof-gated 1-RTT readiness seam. After the managed key schedule verifies the peer Finished, it surfaces the local Finished bytes, publishes `KeysAvailable` for `OneRtt`, and publishes directional 1-RTT packet-protection material in that order. The bridge state rejects premature `OneRtt` key publication unless `PeerFinishedVerified` is already set, which keeps the active application-data seam honest while leaving the supported public boundary unchanged. The runtime still requires the existing Active-phase transition before application streams or application packets can flow.

## Key Components

- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicTlsKeySchedule.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicTransportTlsBridgeState.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicTlsTransportBridgeDriver.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicConnectionRuntime.cs
- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/CRT/REQ-QUIC-CRT-0121.cs
- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/QUIC/REQ-QUIC-API-0009.cs
- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/QUIC/REQ-QUIC-API-0010.cs
- benchmarks/QuicTlsClientFinishedPublicationBenchmarks.cs

## Risks

- If OneRtt keys are published before PeerFinishedVerified, the supported data-path would appear usable before the Finished proof boundary.
- If the bridge gate remains open to premature direct updates, future callers could fabricate client 1-RTT readiness without the peer proof.
- If the benchmark and requirement-home tests do not exercise the client path after certificate-policy acceptance, regressions in the active-loopback seam can be missed.
